era_context: The early 11th century continues with the Song Dynasty in China at the height of its administrative sophistication, the Fatimid Caliphate experiencing internal instability, the Ghaznavid Empire under Mahmud at its zenith, and Western Europe in the early phase of feudal consolidation and monastic reform. This is a period of significant technological advancement (printing in China, maritime trade expansion) and religious ferment (monastic reform movements, pilgrimage culture).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no Old World written records)
Northern and Eastern Europe beyond Rus' and Scandinavia
Southeast Asia (sparse documentation in Western sources)

events.title: Accession of Constantine IX Monomachos as Byzantine Emperor
events.description: Constantine IX Monomachos ascended to the Byzantine throne in 1028 following the death of Constantine VIII. He married the widow Zoe Porphyrogenneta, thereby legitimizing his claim. Constantine IX's reign (1028–1055) was marked by significant military challenges, including conflicts with the Normans in Italy and the Seljuk Turks in Anatolia, as well as the deepening schism between the Eastern Orthodox and Roman Catholic churches. His reign witnessed the beginning of Byzantine military decline in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Michael Psellus, Chronographia (14 Byzantine Emperors), written 11th century
John Skylitzes, Synopsis Historiarum, compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: Constantine IX's accession in 1028 is well-attested in Byzantine chronicles and is not disputed by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Contemporary with Ghaznavid Empire under Mahmud

events.title: Completion of the Brihadesvara Temple in Thanjavur (Chola Empire)
events.description: The Brihadesvara Temple (also called Rajarajesvara Temple) in Thanjavur, South India, was completed under the patronage of the Chola Emperor Rajaraja I (r. 985–1014) or his successor Rajendra I (r. 1014–1044). The temple is one of the greatest achievements of Chola architecture and represents the height of medieval South Indian temple construction. Its completion marked the consolidation of Chola imperial power and the flowering of Chola cultural and religious patronage. The temple's construction spanned approximately 1004–1028 CE.
key_figures: Rajaraja I (Chola Emperor, r. 985–1014, initiator of construction)

sources.name: Chola inscriptions on the temple itself (epigraphic evidence)
Archaeological and architectural analysis of the temple structure
Tamil literary sources (Silappatikaram, Periyapuranam), compiled later but referencing contemporary events

events.certainty_note: The temple's completion date is traditionally assigned to 1028 CE based on epigraphic evidence and architectural analysis, though some sources place it slightly earlier (c. 1010–1025). The construction period is well-attested; the precise completion year is approximate.
cross_references: Part of broader Chola cultural and military expansion under Rajendra I
Contemporary with Byzantine and Islamic cultural achievements

events.title: Normans consolidate control of southern Italy
events.description: Norman mercenaries and settlers, who had begun arriving in southern Italy in the early 11th century, continued to consolidate their control over territories previously held by the Byzantine Empire and Lombard principalities. By 1028, Norman military bands were becoming increasingly organized and were beginning to establish themselves as a permanent military and political force. This process would culminate in the Norman conquest of southern Italy and Sicily over the following decades, fundamentally altering the political geography of the Mediterranean.

sources.name: William of Apulia, Gesta Roberti Wiscardi (Deeds of Robert Guiscard), written late 11th century
Amatus of Montecassino, Historia Normannorum, written 11th century

events.certainty_note: Norman consolidation in southern Italy is well-attested, but the specific events of 1028 are not precisely documented. The process was gradual, and sources often conflate events across multiple years.
cross_references: Precedes the Norman conquest of Sicily (1091)
Contemporary with Byzantine military challenges under Constantine IX

disconfirming_evidence: The completion date of the Brihadesvara Temple is uncertain; some sources place it earlier (c. 1010–1025) or slightly later (c. 1030–1035). The temple's construction period is well-attested, but the precise completion year is conventional. Norman consolidation in southern Italy is a gradual process, and the specific significance of 1028 is not clearly marked in sources; the year is somewhat arbitrary within a broader period of Norman expansion (c. 1015–1050).
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1028 is moderate but regionally uneven. Byzantine sources provide detailed information about Constantine IX's accession. South Indian sources (epigraphic evidence and later Tamil literature) document the Brihadesvara Temple, though dating is approximate. Norman expansion in Italy is documented in later chronicles written decades after events, introducing significant chronological uncertainty. Islamic sources (Ghaznavid, Fatimid) are available but less detailed for this specific year. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe remain poorly documented. The surviving record privileges elite political and religious events over economic and social history.

graph_edges.note: Constantine IX's reign witnessed the deepening of religious tensions that culminated in the Great Schism
The Brihadesvara Temple's completion is part of Rajendra I's broader program of cultural and military expansion
Norman consolidation in southern Italy in the 1020s–1030s led to the conquest of Sicily
